steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which statement is best supported by the text. The key pivot is “However”: while recommender systems generally increase sales, Lee and Hosanagar found that the specific effect differs by product type — item views rose more for utilitarian products, but purchase likelihood rose more for hedonic products. The correct choice must capture this non-uniformity without overstating it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — While recommender systems can affect consumer behavior toward suggested products, those effects are not uniform in nature and strength across product categories.: Directly captures the finding that RS effects differ in kind (views vs. purchases) and direction depending on product category (utilitarian vs. hedonic). ✓
  • Choice B — By directing customers toward specific product categories on a retailer's website, recommender systems may limit the variety of items seen by online shoppers.: Not mentioned in the text — the passage discusses effects on views and purchases, not whether variety is restricted. ✗
  • Choice C — By proposing the most useful options to consumers, recommender systems boost the online visibility of utilitarian items that otherwise tend to be overlooked.: The text says utilitarian items see stronger view effects, but nothing says they are “otherwise overlooked.” Unsupported inference. ✗
  • Choice D — While recommender systems cause consumers to spend more time viewing utilitarian items such as dish detergents, recommender systems' positive effect on purchase rates is likely limited to hedonic items such as music albums.: Overstates the finding. The text says the purchase effect is stronger for hedonic products, not that utilitarian products see no purchase effect at all. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
